text.title: Spring Boot Layered Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for layered Spring Boot 3.x REST APIs.

rule.statement: Controllers MUST accept HTTP requests, call exactly one service method, and return responses. `@Transactional`, repository access, business logic, or multi-service orchestration in a controller is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Thin controllers make tests fast (`@WebMvcTest` + mock service) and prevent layer bleed.

rule.statement: `@Transactional` (or `@Transactional(readOnly=true)`) MUST live on `@Service` methods (or the class). It MUST NOT appear on controllers, repositories, or private methods.
rule.rationale: Transactions on controllers tie HTTP lifecycle to DB lifecycle (open-in-view all over again); private methods are bypassed by the proxy mechanism.

rule.statement: DTOs MUST be Java `record` (Java 17+). Request records carry Bean Validation annotations (`@NotNull`, `@Size`, `@Email`). Service public methods MUST accept/return DTOs, never JPA entities. MapStruct mappers handle entity↔DTO conversion.

rationale.source: Java 17 records spec + Bean Validation 3.x docs

rule.rationale: Records give value semantics + immutability for free; validating at the boundary keeps domain logic clean.

rule.statement: List endpoints MUST accept `org.springframework.data.domain.Pageable` and return `Page<T>` (or `Slice<T>`). Bare `List<T> findAll()` exposed via a controller is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Unpaged endpoints risk OOM under data growth; `Pageable` propagates sort + page metadata to clients.

rule.statement: `@Async` MUST live in a separate Spring bean from the caller (no self-invocation). The async method MUST specify an executor name (`@Async("emailExecutor")`) backed by a named `ThreadPoolTaskExecutor` bean with explicit core/max pool size + queue capacity.

rationale.source: Spring Async docs + observed self-invocation antipattern

rule.rationale: Self-invocation skips the proxy and runs synchronously; default `SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor` spawns unbounded threads.
